title: Theo of Golden
author: Allen Levi
blurb: Theo of Golden is a beautifully crafted story about the power of creative generosity, the importance of wonder to a purposeful life, and the far-reaching possibilities of anonymous kindness.

title: Still Lost: Tales from 2080
author: Sam A. Miller
blurb: A whopping eight rather long short stories are included, featuring such concepts as an alien prostitute, a baboon that gets cyberbullied, an old lady getting killed with a sword, an eight page discussion about grass, and many more!

title: The Correspondent
author: Virginia Evans
blurb: Discover the word-of-mouth hit hailed by Ann Patchett as “A cause for celebration”—an intimate novel about the transformative power of the written word and the beauty of slowing down to reconnect with the people we love.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
blurb: An irresistible interstellar adventure as only Andy Weir could deliver, Project Hail Mary is a tale of discovery, speculation, and survival to rival The Martian—while taking us to places it never dreamed of going.

title: Dear Debbie
author: Freida McFadden
blurb: From #1 New York Times and international bestselling author Freida McFadden comes a biting, subversive thriller about what happens when women finally choose to take justice into their own hands—with killer results.

title: Reminders of Him: A Novel
author: Colleen Hoover
blurb: A troubled young mother yearns for a shot at redemption in this heartbreaking yet hopeful story from #1 New York Times bestselling author Colleen Hoover.

title: The Brothers Karamazov (Hallow Edition)
author: Fyodor Dosoevsky, translated by Constance Garnett
blurb: More than a family drama or murder mystery, this profound philosophical and theological novel wrestles with the deepest human questions: Is God real? What is faith? And what do we owe one another in love and responsibility?

title: The Housemaid
author: Freida McFadden
blurb: An addictive psychological thriller with a jaw-dropping twist. Perfect for fans of Ruth Ware, Lisa Jewell, and Verity.

title: Wuthering Heights (Penguin Classics)
author: Emily Brontë
blurb: Emily Brontë's only novel endures as a work of tremendous and far-reaching influence. The Penguin Classics edition is the definitive version of the text, edited with an introduction by Pauline Nestor.

title: The Housemaid's Secret
author: Freida McFadden
blurb: An unbelievably twisty read that will have you glued to the pages late into the night. Anyone who loves The Woman in the Window, The Wife Between Us and The Girl on the Train will be completely hooked!

title: Sunrise on the Reaping (A Hunger Games Novel)
author: Suzanne Collins
blurb: The phenomenal fifth book in the Hunger Games series! When you’ve been set up to lose everything you love, what is there left to fight for?

title: The Outsiders
author: S. E. Hinton
blurb: The Outsiders is a dramatic and enduring work of fiction that laid the groundwork for the YA genre. S. E. Hinton’s classic story of a boy who finds himself on the outskirts of regular society remains as powerful today as it was the day it was first published.

title: The Giver
author: Lois Lowry
blurb: Life in the community where Jonas lives is idyllic. Designated birthmothers produce newchildren, who are assigned to appropriate family units. Citizens are assigned their partners and their jobs. No one thinks to ask questions. Everyone obeys. Everyone is the same. Except Jonas.

title: Lord of the Flies
author: William Golding
blurb: At the dawn of the next world war, a plane crashes on an uncharted island, stranding a group of schoolboys. At first, with no adult supervision, their freedom is something to celebrate. This far from civilization they can do anything they want. Anything. But as order collapses, as strange howls echo in the night, as terror begins its reign, the hope of adventure seems as far removed from reality as the hope of being rescued.

title: Hatchet
author: Gary Paulsen
publisher: Simon & Schuster Books for Young Readers
blurb: Thirteen-year-old Brian Robeson, haunted by his secret knowledge of his mother’s infidelity, is traveling by single-engine plane to visit his father for the first time since the divorce. When the plane crashes, killing the pilot, the sole survivor is Brian. He is alone in the Canadian wilderness with nothing but his clothing, a tattered windbreaker, and the hatchet his mother had given him as a present.

title: I Love you to the Moon and Back
author: Amelia Hepworth and Tim Warnes
blurb: Show your child just how strong your love is every minute of the day with this sweet, gentle rhyme that's perfect for sharing. Now in a jacketed hardcover format!

title: The Amazing Generation: Your Guide to Fun and Freedom in a Screen-Filled World
author: Jonathan Haidt and Catherine Price
blurb: Inspired by the #1 New York Times bestseller The Anxious Generation, this new book for kids and tweens is a comprehensive guide for living a happy and exciting life that isn’t hijacked by a smartphone.

title: How to Catch a Leprechaun
author: Adam Wallace
blurb: Celebrate St. Patrick's Day with the #1 New York Times and USA Today Bestselling Laugh-out-loud Holiday Adventure for Kids!

title: The Very Hungry Caterpillar
author: Eric Carle
blurb: Follow along as one of nature’s loveliest marvels eats his way through an amazing variety of foods as he prepares to grow into a beautiful butterfly! Children will love the strikingly bold, colorful pictures against a simple text in large, clear type.

title: See You Later, Alligator
author: Sally Hopgood
blurb: A departing tortoise has his bags packed and is almost ready to set out on an adventure, but he can’t leave until he says good-bye to each of his animal friends. The tortoise stops for every last timid mouse and bumblebee, shouting his rhyming good-byes, making you wonder if, perhaps, he’s stalling the start of his trip.

title: If There Is No God: The Battle Over Who Defines Good and Evil
author: Dennis Prager
blurb: One of the most important conservative voices of the last century takes on the dangers of secularism, perhaps the most important issue in our lives.

title: If the Tree Could Speak: The Story of the Cross That Saw It All
author: Tim Tebow
blurb: In If the Tree Could Speak, New York Times bestselling author Tim Tebow invites you into the story of Jesus’ crucifixion, told from the perspective of the closest thing to Jesus on that day—the wooden cross.

title: Hormone Havoc: A Science-Backed Protocol for Perimenopause and Menopause
author: Amy Shah MD
blurb: Doctor, author, and influencer (@DrAmyShah) Amy Shah, MD, shares a nutritional, science-based protocol to minimize hormonal havoc, hot flashes, and night sweats and take on perimenopause and menopause with improved mood, energy, and health.

title: Forgotten Home Apothecary : 250 Powerful Remedies at Your Fingertips
author: Dr. Nicole Apelian
blurb: This is the only holistic medicine book out there where you’ll find the time-tested natural remedies our grandparents relied on, back when apothecaries hadn’t yet been replaced by pharmacies.

title: Murdle: Volume 1
author: G. T. Karber
blurb: G. T. Karber, the creator of the popular online daily mystery game Murdle, presents the first collection of 100 original murder mystery logic puzzles―a perfect gift for armchair detectives and puzzlers to hone their minds and solve a series of crimes.

title: The Let Them Theory: A Life-Changing Tool That Millions of People Can't Stop Talking About
author: Mel Robbins and Sawyer Robbins
blurb: Two simple words—Let Them—will set you free. Free from the opinions, drama, and judgments of others. Free from the exhausting cycle of trying to manage everything and everyone around you. The Let Them Theory puts the power to create a life you love back in your hands—and this book will show you exactly how to do it.

title: Atomic Habits
author: James Clear
blurb: No matter your goals, Atomic Habits offers a proven framework for improving—every day. James Clear, one of the world’s leading experts on habit formation, reveals practical strategies that will teach you exactly how to form good habits, break bad ones, and master the tiny behaviors that lead to remarkable results.

title: The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma
author: Bessel van der Kolk M.D.
blurb: In The Body Keeps the Score, Dr. van der Kolk uses recent scientific advances to show how trauma literally reshapes both body and brain, compromising sufferers’ capacities for pleasure, engagement, self-control, and trust.

title: The Anxious Generation: How the Great Rewiring of Childhood Is Causing an Epidemic of Mental Illness
author: Jonathan Haidt
blurb: Haidt has spent his career speaking truth backed by data in the most difficult landscapes—communities polarized by politics and religion, campuses battling culture wars, and now the public health emergency faced by Gen Z. We cannot afford to ignore his findings about protecting our children—and ourselves—from the psychological damage of a phone-based life.

title: The Four Agreements: A Practical Guide to Personal Freedom
author: Don Miguel Ruiz
blurb: Based on ancient Toltec wisdom, The Four Agreements offer a powerful code of conduct that can rapidly transform our lives to a new experience of freedom, true happiness, and love.

title: Stripped Down: Unfiltered and Unapologetic
author: Bunnie Xo
blurb: Stripped Down is the story of how Bunnie Xo rose to the top, how she used her own wiles to reach her goals, how she knew redemption was up to her—and that no one could hand it to her—and a message to anyone who needs advice on breaking their own cycles.

title: A World Appears: A Journey into Consciousness
author: Michael Pollan
blurb: From the #1 New York Times bestselling author of How to Change Your Mind, a panoptic exploration of consciousness—what it is, who has it, and why—and a meditation on the essence of our humanity.

title: Man's Search for Meaning
author: Viktor E. Frankl
blurb: A book for finding purpose and strength in times of great despair, the international best-seller is still just as relevant today as when it was first published.

title: Once Upon a Time: The Captivating Life of Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy
author: Elizabeth Beller
blurb: Based on in-depth research and exclusive interviews with friends, family members, teachers, roommates, and colleagues, and featuring never-before-seen family photos, this comprehensive biography reveals a multifaceted woman worthy of our attention regardless of her husband and untimely death.

title: The Greatest Sentence Ever Written
author: Walter Isaacson
blurb: America’s bestselling biographer reveals the origins of the most revolutionary sentence in the Declaration of Independence, the one that defines who we are as Americans—and explains how it should shape our politics today.

title: Nobody's Girl: A Memoir of Surviving Abuse and Fighting for Justice
author: Virginia Roberts Giuffre
blurb: Nobody’s Girl is an astonishing affirmation of the late Virginia Roberts Giuffre’s unshakable will—first, to claw her way out of victimhood, and then to shine light on wrongdoing and fight for a safer, fairer world. Equal parts intimate and fierce, it is a remarkable narrative of fortitude in the face of depravity and despair.

title: We the Women: The Hidden Heroes Who Shaped America
author: Norah O'Donnell and Kate Anderson Brower
blurb: A vivid portrait of the unsung American women from 1776 to today who changed the course of history in their fight for freedom and helped shape a more perfect union.

title: The Psychology of Money: Timeless lessons on wealth, greed, and happiness
author: Morgan Housel
blurb: In The Psychology of Money, award-winning author Morgan Housel shares 19 short stories exploring the different ways people think about money and teaches you how to make better sense of one of life's most important topics.

title: Young Man in a Hurry: A Memoir of Discovery
author: Gavin Newsom
blurb: From California Governor Gavin Newsom comes an intimate and poignant account of identity, belonging, and the defining moments that inspired a life in politics.

title: Manufacturing Delusion: How the Left Uses Brainwashing, Indoctrination, and Propaganda Against You
author: Buck Sexton
blurb: An unnerving explanation of how Orwell’s 1984 could become reality, Manufacturing Delusion is essential reading for anyone who wants to know how to stand strong when everyone around them is going crazy.

title: Stop, in the Name of God: Why Honoring the Sabbath Will Transform Your Life
author: Charlie Kirk
blurb: Through Stop in the Name of God, bestselling author Charlie Kirk guides you on how to unplug, recharge, and reconnect with God, family, and yourself in a way that nurtures your soul. In a world dominated by screens and constant noise, Kirk presents the Sabbath as a radical act of resistance.

title: The Only Living Trusts Book You’ll Ever Need: How to Make Your Own Living Trust, Avoid Probate & Protect Your Heirs
author: Garrett Monroe
blurb: Don’t leave the future of your estate up to chance. This is your toolkit to ensure your assets are managed exactly as you wish, without the interference of courts or the burden of unnecessary taxes.

title: The 48 Laws of Power
author: Robert Greene
blurb: Some laws teach the need for prudence ("Law 1: Never Outshine the Master"), others teach the value of confidence ("Law 28: Enter Action with Boldness"), and many recommend absolute self-preservation ("Law 15: Crush Your Enemy Totally"). Every law, though, has one thing in common: an interest in total domination. In a bold and arresting two-color backage, The 48 Laws of Power is ideal whether your aim is conquest, self-defense, or simply to understand the rules of the game.

title: Black AF History: The Un-Whitewashed Story of America
author: Michael Harriot
blurb: From acclaimed columnist and political commentator Michael Harriot, a searingly smart and bitingly hilarious retelling of American history that corrects the record and showcases the perspectives and experiences of Black Americans.

title: How to Test Negative for Stupid: And Why Washington Never Will
author: John Kennedy
blurb: One of the most distinctive and funny politicians, Senator John Kennedy (the one from Louisiana) offers his perceptive (and hilarious) takes on the ridiculousness of political life in this scathingly witty takedown of Washington and its elite denizens.

title: Felicia's Favorites: A Novel
author: Danielle Steel
blurb:
A mother bestows a life-changing legacy on her five adult daughters in this profoundly moving novel from #1 New York Times bestselling author Danielle Steel.

After the unexpected death of their mother, Felicia Morgan Weston, her five daughters are summoned to a historic Connecticut farmhouse for the reading of her will. Still reeling from shock, they hear revelations that will potentially change their lives—and they realize there was much more to their mother than they ever knew.

Charlotte, the oldest, always resented her mother’s advice, but now misses her terribly. An entrepreneur and single mother, she doesn’t dare hope for a second chance at love.

Although content with her career as a TV producer and her life with her partner in Greenwich Village, Quinne is about to have an opportunity to dream bigger.

Former ballet dancer Olivia has lived as a paraplegic since a car accident twelve years ago. Refusing to be a burden, she has denied herself the love of her life.

Despite her mother’s disapproval, Veronica resigned herself to a secret relationship with an ambitious married senator.

Happily married mother of three Isabelle has just found out that her husband is having an affair with his much younger intern.

Each sister is about to receive a gift beyond her wildest dreams from their very private but loving mother, who considered all her girls her favorites. Danielle Steel’s new novel is a moving testament to the power of a mother’s love and the importance of fully embracing life.

title: Hide: Poems
author: Carolina Ebeid
blurb:
A reinvention of visual poetry and personal history charting exile’s impact on memory, identity, and futurity

Intellectual and intimate, Carolina Ebeid's Hide gathers shreds of memory, dream, and the ordinary artifacts of diaspora, as the poet casts a sounding line into her patrilineal and matrilineal histories in Palestine and Cuba. With the hum of cassettes and the glow of projectors, these poems superimpose voice upon voice, image upon image, a here upon a there, to disclose the choral noise inside postmemory.

Hide is a restless innovation of form and multimodal expression breaking open words across Arabic, English, and Spanish to release hidden meanings. Poems trace the letter M back to the Phoenician pictograph of waves, while technological “glitches” are portals that summon oracular voices across the family archive. In swirling “spell” poems, Ebeid conjures Cuban American artist Ana Mendieta, whose Siluetas write the human shape upon the earth.

Ebeid’s title is prismatic: Hide as in concealment, as in animal skin, as in to secret oneself away. Hide commands attention like a whispering voice, prompting readers to lean in, to listen for transmissions from ancestors and futurity both.

title: Her Muddy Majesty of Muck
author: Beatrice Alemagna
blurb:
A little girl’s too-big feelings spur a cathartic journey of adventure and discovery, in this picture book for kids ages 5–8, perfect for fans of Where the Wild Things Are.

Yuki is lost in a dark mood on her way home from school. In a flash of frustration, she tosses her house keys down an open manhole. When she climbs down to retrieve them, she discovers another world: Mudland.

The Mud Princess, an enormous dripping thing made of mud and sticks, greets Yuki and shows her the wonders of her kingdom. Yuki’s dark mood is at home here—her sadness and anger fit right in. As they journey together, the Mud Princess becomes larger, absorbing the anger around her. Everything the princess touches becomes muddy, even Yuki. Yuki faces a choice—to become a Mud Princess herself or to return to the world above ground and its expectations.

As in her other picture books, Beatrice Alemagna explores children’s feelings in a way that speaks to them directly. Her Muddy Majesty of Muck explores anger and frustration with humor and brings us two unforgettable characters, the relatable Yuki and the strange and fascinating Mud Princess.

title: Simple Sabotage Field Manual: Methods to Quietly Undermine Hostile Governments, Military Forces, and Industrial Operations
author: the Office of Strategic Services
blurb:
Originally produced in 1944 by the United States Office of Strategic Services (OSS)—the wartime intelligence agency that later evolved into the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA)—the Simple Sabotage Field Manual is a remarkable artifact of covert warfare.

Designed for distribution among resistance groups, sympathetic civilians, and undercover operatives in enemy-occupied territories, the manual outlines practical, low-risk ways for ordinary individuals to quietly undermine hostile governments, military forces, and industrial operations.

Unlike traditional wartime sabotage involving explosives or specialized training, this guide focuses on subtle, everyday actions—small decisions, deliberate inefficiencies, and minor disruptions that, when multiplied across a population, can significantly degrade an opponent’s productivity and morale. The manual explains how simple behaviors such as misfiling documents, creating bureaucratic delays, performing work poorly, spreading confusion, and fostering interpersonal friction can collectively hinder the functioning of an organization far more effectively than dramatic acts of destruction.

title: The Coming Food Crisis: How Corporations, Activists, and Climate Alarmists Are Waging War on Farmers
subgroup: Bruce Wagner's Pick of the Week
author: John Klar
blurb:
They’re coming for your food.

Years ago, there was a cult classic sci-fi film called Soylent Green, about Big Ag going big aggro and secretly processing human flesh as cheap food. Of course, John Klar’s new book isn’t about that—but does wade into the same muddy waters of multinational corporations, animal rights extremists, climate crusaders, and chemical saturation’s relentless war on America’s farms.

John Klar is an attorney-turned-homesteader, grass-fed beef farmer and food rights activist—he writes and farms from the same Vermont mountaintop his family has called home for nine generations—and knows whereof he speaks. In The Coming Food Crisis, Klar chronicles the deadly harvest that each day signs the death warrant on dynasties of national farms. The heartland that is part of the mythology of America is on the way out. It’s only a matter of time before farms as we know them go the way of payphone and record players, replaced by industrially tilled soils sickened by chemical applications, evaporating underground aquifers, and the erasure of pollinators. Read this book and weep—then do something about it. Klar tells you how.

title: The Coming Food Crisis: How Corporations, Activists, and Climate Alarmists Are Waging War on Farmers
author: John Klar
blurb:
John Klar is an attorney-turned-homesteader and grass-fed beef farmer, a food rights activist, and a fervent member of the MAHA movement. Klar’s book Small Farm Republic: Why Conservatives Must Embrace Local Agriculture, Reject Climate Alarmism, and Lead an Environmental Revival advocated for a conservative environmental political platform premised on local, regenerative agriculture. John writes and farms from the same Vermont mountaintop his family has called home for nine generations.

“The Coming Food Crisis shines a light on the vulnerabilities of our current food system and the farmers caught in the middle. Klar traces the forces reshaping our food supply in a way that’s both gripping and deeply human. His analysis invites readers to question assumptions and look beyond the supermarket aisles to the soil itself. It’s a timely, compelling read for anyone seeking a deeper understanding of where food, health, and freedom intersect.”
—Dr. Sina McCullough, author of Hands Off My Food! and Beyond Labels

An Excerpt from The Coming Food Crisis

The Green Revolution: The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly

The so-called “Green Revolution” of the twentieth century refers to the global proliferation of industrial agriculture following World War II. Agricultural production underwent a rapid and widespread shift from traditional methods to reliance on new hybrid crop strains, mechanization, expanded irrigation infrastructure, and the use of chemical fertilizers, herbicides, and pesticides. These shifts profoundly increased crop yields in the short term. They lifted millions of people out of poverty, but over time, productivity has declined as the hidden environmental and health costs of these practices have become evident.

The benefits of industrial food production were soon visible—increased yields of cereal crops supplied meals to millions and allowed many nations to become net food exporters rather than importers, thereby boosting their economies and food security simultaneously. Poverty was widely eradicated, real incomes rose, infant mortality rates declined, and more food was grown on less land. The putative “father” of the Green Revolution, Norman Borlaug, was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1970, credited with saving over a billion human lives.

The modern world of agricultural technology is truly astounding. Genetic engineering, hormones, antibiotics, and other scientific advances have joined a fossil-fuel-powered array of mechanical marvels that surpass the wildest imaginings of science fiction. Magnificent computer-guided tractors with sophisticated attachments plow the ground, seed vast regions, spray fertilizers, insecticides, and specially formulated chemicals, and harvest a bounty unimaginable a century ago. Specialty machines have been designed for harvesting corn, wheat, berries, watermelons, and greens—a world of technology nearly as varied as the crops available to grow. Irrigation, processing, and distribution networks similarly impress the human imagination.

But this fantastic bounty comes with a tremendous cost. Industrially tilled soils are sickly and eroding. Precious underground water aquifers have been slurped up in dry regions, evaporating rapidly on dying soils. Chemical applications have sickened the soil microbiome, depleted vital minerals, and killed pollinators.5 Human health is deteriorating as man-made food products tainted with chemicals and deprived of nutritional value have replaced natural provisions.

As is so often the case with technological innovation, the downsides of this revolution were not immediately evident. Because the chief motivation of agricultural conversion was to fill empty stomachs, food quality and ecosystem health were not prioritized. Modern crops, especially those reliant on GMO-dependent monocultures, including tens of millions of acres of corn and soy, rely heavily on synthetic fertilizers, which deplete soils and water resources. The chemical applications (especially pesticides, glyphosate, and herbicides) upon which they are dependent inflict ecological destruction.

The ugly truth of the Green Revolution is that seemingly miraculous productivity gains have masked numerous problems that far outweigh the supposed benefits. Compacted soils reduce tilth and yield; chemical applications weaken soils, accelerating erosion and necessitating ever-greater applications of fertilizers and other chemicals. Roughly half of the world’s food production is now dependent on synthetic nitrogen fertilizer use.7

Genetic modifications permitted an even greater extension of these perceived production benefits, adding glyphosate as a chemical dependency to the supposedly salvific industrial food production system. America industry, university researchers, and government agencies all pushed US food production toward greater “efficiency” and yields, sacrificing crop biodiversity and local agrarian cultures on the altars of profit and technological progress. Large-scale agricultural production increased farm consolidation. Only larger enterprises had access to credit (often from foreign investors) and could take advantage of bulk purchasing power.

The consolidation of food production, processing, and distribution into an ever-shrinking club of enormously powerful corporations or massive farms diminishes local food supplies and local food security. It also centralizes food production, creating a dependency on fossil fuel energy—diesel and gasoline to power tractors and equipment, and natural gas to manufacture synthetic fertilizers.

This arrangement worked economically as long as oil and other fuels remained cost-effective. Rising fertilizer costs (which spiked sharply during the COVID-19 pandemic) have already begun to reverse those favorable economics. Over the decades, industrial crop yields have declined, necessitating ever-higher applications of fertilizers and other inputs to maintain yields. Pesticides, herbicides, and defoliants, as well as agricultural machinery and irrigation systems, are all derived from and dependent upon crude oil for their manufacture.

Industrial fertilizers have dramatically exacerbated environmental problems, especially from synthetic urea—excess urea results in emissions of nitrous oxide, which is considered by the greenhouse gas metric to be 265 times more potent than carbon dioxide. Water quality and quantity have both suffered significantly from the Green Revolution, as chemicals pollute drinking water and aquifers are depleted far more quickly than nature can replenish them.

Corporations profited tremendously from the Green Revolution, but human and environmental health have been dangerously compromised. Commodity output and economic growth increased at the expense of soil, water, food wholesomeness, and food security. Long-standing regenerative farming practices were abandoned, chemicals were spewed recklessly, and water supplies were gobbled up shortsightedly. Unsurprisingly, yield rates have steadily declined, necessitating the use of more land, chemicals, and fossil fuels.

Inexpensive, plentiful food supplies are thus a short-term illusion, constructed atop a fragile dependency on finite energy sources that are steadily becoming more costly. The system is both economically and environmentally unsustainable, as well as unhealthy for humans and the ecosystem. Yet, this unnatural production model is enthusiastically embraced by the cadre of corporate profiteers who have led the charge to “save the world” with gimmicks and deceptions about agriculture. These same forces now conspire to pressure the entire world’s agricultural production into this toxic, food-dominating model in the name of mitigating climate change and averting global famine. This consolidation increases the long-term risks of famine.

The greatest cost of this seemingly wondrous industrial transition remains concealed from most people’s awareness: dependency on these technologies, replacing the once-universal local connection between humans and soil. When God evicted Adam and Eve from the Garden of Eden, He left them an umbilical cord to the life-giving Earth. That nurturing connection has been effectively severed by the radical modern shift from a direct relationship with soil, plants, and animals to a dependency on cheap oil pumped from deserts or ocean floors far away, on computer chips manufactured in Taiwan, on distant, impersonal supply lines, and on processing and preservatives.

Corporate America, now sitting at the table of the World Economic Forum in partnership with the United Nations and the World Health Organization, seeks to make the final connection from its New World Order factories to our human navels. Dazzled by the technology and ease promised, humanity has taken the proverbial bait at great peril.

Once disconnected from rich soils and nutrient-dense foods, humans become not only physically ill but also alienated from other vital connections. Wendell Berry correctly observes that “As industrial technology advances and enlarges, and in the process assumes greater social, economic, and political force, it carries people away from where they belong by history, culture, deeds, association, and affection.” This severance unmoors modern human populations from their ability to reclaim their own food production, including cultural knowledge, community, rural lands, livestock, seed genetics, and the tools and other resources required to grow their own food.

The journey to rediscover the primacy of food in human health is a homecoming to agriculture, its rich history, complex economics, vibrant community, and profound meaning. It is not just food that has set modern Americans adrift: it is the alienation from farming and food culture, the loss of rural communities and economic sustenance, and the abandonment of agrarian life in favor of techno-urban alienation and confinement. Most importantly, it is an utter surrender of self-determination and liberty to corporate-government domination.
